title: What an American thinks about Nepal

transcript.text: Okay, this video is what an American thinks about Nepal. Okay, I was in Nepal for five and a half months, but anyways, what I think about Nepal. Nepalese are very, very patient. I mean, this is kind of a thing with third worlders, but I really noticed it with Nepal, maybe because it was the first place that I saw the third world country. I spent a long time, but Nepalese will hang out forever.

Like, you'll have Nepal. I stayed at this massage, Thai Massage Institute, Center of Kathmandu, Center of Healing, which I guess now is called Healing Hand Center, but it was in a really cool place, like a house. Really cool house. And there would be like these Nepalese who'd hang out and just like hang out. They'd like sit there with you and just sit there all day long, hang out.

You know, that's kind of the thing about third world countries is people just hang out forever because they don't have jobs. So they'll just kind of sit there and they're really friendly to like really good, you know, soft energy. You know, you'd be like, how's it going? The Indians do this. How's it going? They'd be like, this means like, oh, you know, whatever. It can mean like everything. Like, how's it going? Good.

You're like, you know, are you bored? I don't know. But they're really friendly. They kind of know like English. They have pretty good English. The Nepalese, you know, they all speak English in India because I was like an English commonwealth, English outpost, whatever colony.

But I don't think the English ever actually controlled Nepal like they did in England. So the Nepalese are kind of more, uh, not as, I've never actually went to India. I got the visa, but I heard they're just cooler. They're like more mellow. They like you more of it.

Um, and they're really friendly. Like I had a friend who gave me a big old bag of weed, just gave it to me that I made like 207 joints out of. And there's weed everywhere there. Like there's this road between this hotel I was staying and the gym I went to when I was there.

Um, and the entire road. It's like right in the middle of the city. It's the road you take to go to Pocara. The whole thing is lined with huge marijuana plants. Like packed. So thick you can't even walk in there and they're like 10 feet high.

And I bet you if you uprooted any one of those you get high for ass. Like I found this big old marijuana plant right behind my hotel room right outside my window. And I just uprooted it and I was smoking on it for like two months.

Um, and, but, but, but a cool thing about Napoli is also is, you know, the trekking home. There are these, uh, all these, uh, like the mountains are very, very, very, uh, hilly and very, very, very steep and, and, and, uh, and lots of trees on it and very, very populated with lots of villages in there, but no roads at all.
It's like, it's like, swear to God, it's the best. If you want an experience of going back to like ancient days, like thousands of years ago and do a trek in Nepal, like the anniversary.

Cause it's like, they have these perfectly path, stone paths. And then between the scenes, it's more like kind of like paths ways that you only walk to that not even a car could drive because it's just, it's just a path.

You know, and not even motorcycles around it. Just, you could just walk there, but then you get to these villages, like cities, like in the middle of the mountains that you can only walk to that are like days walk away, like days and days and days walk from the civilization.
And there's entire towns up there. It's crazy.
And so, you know, they'd have like main street of the town, but instead of main street, it's like main path.
It's wild.
And there are lots of, uh, you know, hostels for the tourists to stay at, but, and the only town that actually had cars was, uh, Johnson.
But the only way the cars could get there was like people like flying cars in.
There was no road to there.
And there were only like one car, you know, and like a couple of motorcycles, you know, cause there was like an airport, but that was wild.
Um, but they're really friendly, like little kids like chasing after you, you know, and they see you really friendly.
And even though there were so many tourists walking through, they were still acting like, you know, like they, like, like it was novelty to see tourists.
But of course I was on my bike, which made me a way novelty.
So I was like the only tourist on a bike there.
But, um, they're very, uh, reminiscent.
Anyways, yeah, um, very unfriendly.
Friendly people.
Um, they, you know, like, like my Nepalese friend who bought, gave me all that weed, like his parents invited us over to have a party.
And they, uh, they were a potluck and they set up this entire elaborate potluck for us and gave us alcohol.
I don't think they even, I think, yeah, we chipped in to pay for it.
But I remember I got, we had all, got all drunk and puked on their roof.
And they were just like, they're like watching us, like, like we were, you know, being cool, like we were entertaining them.
Um, but, uh, a lot of history there, like lots of old, um, um, old buildings from like the palace, you know, palace area.
It's like old stratified, uh, like there's one that had all these different layers you go up.
Just to get some challenges to the least of it, which I guess where the royal family lived a long time ago.
Um, and, uh, yeah, it was pretty, uh, really pretty.
About history, they had this little girl there who was like nine and was supposed to be a goddess.
Like a reincarnation of a goddess.
My mom went out there and visited me and we went out and looked at it.
And the girl, and she'd like stand outside and there'd all be this, all these like tourists.
And even Nepalese there, I guess you're supposed to be blessed if you see her maybe even.
And she just like stood there, kind of like bored, like, I gotta do this.
And then I went back in.
I guess they choose that she's a goddess because she doesn't have any freckles on her body or something.
So yeah, but there were lots of car wrecks.
Like they didn't know how to drive.
Like every week you'd see a new photo of like a overturned truck on the road from Pokhara to, uh, Katmandir and people dying and stuff.
Luckily I didn't feel like I was almost killed when I rode my bike out to Pokhara.
But, um, they, they go slower though.
So you can grab on the back of the trucks as you go up the hill.
But, um, yeah.
And then there was this also, they had like, yeah, just lots of weird stuff there.
You know, they have like a nunnery there and a, um, I guess 30% were Buddhists and 70% were Hindu.
But they had like a Buddhist nunnery there down the hill from the male monastery, which was really pretty up on top of the hill.
And then they had this like Tibetan lady there who was like supposed to be like a witch doctor who'd like give you blessings and stuff.
And she made me drink her spit.
I shouldn't have done it because she was a bitch too.
She, she slapped me in my face and like yelled at me.
I went there because I had asthma.
She said, she was slapping me and yelling at me and telling me to drink the spit.
And I was just like, get out of here.
I was like, now give me my money and get out of here.
We had to wait like an hour just to see her.
It was like, because there was like hundreds of people there waiting and with some friends.
And they were like, oh, she had good energy though.
I'm like, drinking some old lady spit.
Um, I think she was taking a piss.
But, um, um, you know, the traffic there is really interesting.
I've never been to India, but the traffic in Nepal was interesting, man.
Cause you'd have these big roundabouts with no light and people would just go in there and it'd be like every man for himself, dude.
It was like, you know, that's how I am on my bike everywhere I go.
So, you know, you just blow through the stop sign and it's just every man for themselves.
But, you know, they had it worked out cause it was a roundabout.
So you just jump in there and have your way.
But even in this, in the, uh, in the, um, intersections, it was like that.
You just blow through and just dodge everybody.
Um, but you know, it works out better there than here cause they drive these little rickshaws.
So they're a lot smaller.
So there's less space you take up as you do that.
It might be harder if everybody's in a normal car, but, um, it's cool.
Works out well, you know.
Um, so anyways, it's what an American thinks about Nepal.
